title: Food-Mood Thought Record
description: Use this worksheet to identify and examine thoughts about food, eating, and nutrition that may be affecting your mood or creating unnecessary stress.
instructions: When you notice strong thoughts or emotional reactions related to food, eating choices, or nutrition — complete each section below to examine the connection between your thoughts, emotions, and eating patterns.

fields.label: 1. Situation — What happened? (e.g., choosing what to eat, after eating, reading about nutrition)
fields.placeholder: Describe the food or nutrition situation that triggered your reaction...

fields.label: 2. Automatic Thought — What went through your mind about food or eating?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'I've ruined my diet', 'I should never eat that', 'I'm not doing nutrition right'

fields.label: 3. Mood Impact — How much did this thought affect your mood? (0 = not at all, 100 = extremely)
4. Emotions — What did you feel? (e.g., guilt, anxiety, frustration, shame)
5. Thinking Pattern — Which pattern best fits?

options.label: All-or-Nothing ('I ate one cookie, so the whole day is ruined')
Should Statements ('I should only eat perfectly healthy food')
Catastrophizing ('This meal will undo all my progress')
Labeling ('I have no willpower', 'I'm bad with food')
Emotional Reasoning ('I feel stressed so I need comfort food')
Overgeneralization ('I always make bad food choices')

fields.label: 6. Evidence For — What supports this thought?
7. Evidence Against — What contradicts this thought? Consider the bigger picture.
fields.placeholder: Is one meal really that impactful? What do you know about how nutrition actually works over time?

fields.label: 8. Balanced Thought — What is a more helpful, realistic perspective?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'One meal doesn't define my nutrition. Overall patterns matter more than individual choices.'

fields.label: 9. Re-rate Mood Impact — How much is this thought affecting your mood now? (0 = not at all, 100 = extremely)

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for professional nutrition counseling or mental health treatment. If you have a complicated relationship with food, consider working with a registered dietitian or therapist who specializes in disordered eating. If you are in crisis, call or text 988.
